---
title: "Phase 3: GWAS Mapping"
subtitle: "GRM construction, variance checking, and association mapping"
date: last-modified
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
---

## Overview

The third phase of the pipeline maps simulated phenotypes
using GCTA to evaluate GWA power and precision. It takes the
PLINK filesets from Phase 2 and produces per-marker association statistics under four mapping conditions.

The processing flow is:

```
PLINK binaries (from Phase 2)
 │
 ├─ GCTA_MAKE_GRM ──────────► GRM binaries + check_vp.hsq
 │                                   │
 ▼                                   ▼
 PYTHON_CHECK_VP ────────────► variance-checked .pheno file
 │
 ▼
 GCTA_PERFORM_GWA ───────────► .fastGWA or .mlma association results
                                 │
                                 ├─ Phase 4: QTL Detection & Assessment
                                 └─ DB Migration: write_gwa_to_db.R
```

Each process is described below with the exact commands run, key parameters,
and links to external tool documentation.

**Mode and type parameterization:** The mapping phase is parameterized by two
variables defined in `main.nf`:

- **mode**: `"inbred"` or `"loco"` &mdash; determines the type of GRM and the
  GWA algorithm. These lowercase values are the canonical `algorithm` identifiers
  used in database mapping IDs.
- **type**: `"pca"` or `"nopca"` &mdash; determines whether the first
  principal component eigenvector is included as a covariate. `type == "pca"`
  maps to `pca = TRUE` in the database mapping ID.

```nextflow
ch_mode = Channel.of(
    ["inbred", "fastGWA"],
    ["loco", "mlma"]
)

ch_type = Channel.of(
    "pca",
    "nopca"
)
```

Each simulation replicate is mapped under all four combinations
(`inbred x pca`, `inbred x nopca`, `loco x pca`, `loco x nopca`), driven by
Nextflow channel `combine` operations. The `mode` crossing happens at
`GCTA_MAKE_GRM` and the `type` crossing happens at `GCTA_PERFORM_GWA`.


## 1. GCTA_MAKE_GRM

**Source:** `modules/gcta/make_grm/main.nf`

Builds a genetic relatedness matrix (GRM) and estimates phenotypic variance via
REML. This process runs two sequential GCTA commands.

### Step 1: Build the GRM

The GRM construction method depends on the `mode` parameter:

```bash
if [[ ${mode} == "inbred" ]]; then
    GRM_OPTION="--make-grm-inbred"
else
    GRM_OPTION="--make-grm"
fi

gcta64 --bfile TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group} \
        --autosome --maf ${maf} ${GRM_OPTION} \
        --out TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_gcta_grm_${mode} \
        --thread-num 1
```

| Flag | Purpose | Docs |
|------|---------|------|
| `--make-grm-inbred` | Construct a kinship matrix for inbred organisms, adjusting for reduced heterozygosity | [GCTA GRM](https://yanglab.westlake.edu.cn/software/gcta/#MakingaGRM) |
| `--make-grm` | Construct a standard genome-wide relatedness matrix | [GCTA GRM](https://yanglab.westlake.edu.cn/software/gcta/#MakingaGRM) |
| `--autosome` | Use only autosomal markers | [GCTA GRM](https://yanglab.westlake.edu.cn/software/gcta/#MakingaGRM) |
| `--maf` | Filter markers below the minor allele frequency threshold | [GCTA GRM](https://yanglab.westlake.edu.cn/software/gcta/#MakingaGRM) |

- **`mode == "inbred"`**: Uses `--make-grm-inbred`, which constructs a kinship
  matrix tailored for populations of inbred organisms. The inbred GRM adjusts
  relatedness estimates to account for the reduced heterozygosity characteristic
  of inbred lines.
- **`mode == "loco"`**: Uses `--make-grm`, which constructs a standard
  genome-wide relatedness matrix using all autosomal markers.

Because `main.nf` defines `ch_mode` with both `["inbred", "fastGWA"]` and
`["loco", "mlma"]`, each simulation replicate produces two GRMs &mdash; one
inbred and one standard.

### Step 2: REML variance estimation

```bash
gcta64 --grm TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_gcta_grm_${mode} \
        --pheno ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sims.phen \
        --reml --out check_vp \
        --thread-num 1
```

This estimates the phenotypic variance (Vp) from the simulated phenotype data
using restricted maximum likelihood (REML). See
[GCTA GREML analysis documentation](https://yanglab.westlake.edu.cn/software/gcta/#GREMLanalysis).

| Flag | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `--grm` | Input GRM prefix from step 1 |
| `--pheno` | Simulated phenotype file |
| `--reml` | Perform REML analysis to estimate variance components |

### Example `check_vp.hsq` output

From test data (`data/test_data/.../GCTA_MAKE_GRM/check_vp.hsq`):

```
Source	Variance	SE
V(G)	46.564277	40.308593
V(e)	158.873235	39.887704
Vp	205.437512	30.661982
V(G)/Vp	0.226659	0.185504
logL	-301.643
logL0	-302.603
LRT	1.920
df	1
Pval	8.2935e-02
n	96
```

The key value is **Vp** (total phenotypic variance), which is checked in the
next process.

### Inputs / Outputs

| Direction | File | Description |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| Input | `TO_SIMS_*.bed/.bim/.fam` | Phenotype-annotated PLINK binary fileset from Phase 2 |
| Input | `*_sims.phen` + `*_sims.par` | Phenotype and parameter files from Phase 2 |
| Input | `mode` (val) | `"inbred"` or `"loco"` |
| Output | `*_gcta_grm_${mode}.grm.bin/.grm.N.bin/.grm.id` | GRM binary files |
| Output | `check_vp.hsq` | REML variance component estimates |
| Output | PLINK files + phenotype files | Passed through for downstream use |


## 2. PYTHON_CHECK_VP

**Source:** `modules/python/check_vp.nf`

Inspects the estimated phenotypic variance (Vp) from the `check_vp.hsq` file
produced by GCTA REML. This step ensures that simulated phenotypes exhibit
sufficient variance, preventing near-zero variance from causing numerical
issues in the association mapping step.

**Command:**

```bash
python3 check_vp.py --check_vp ${hsq_in} --simulated_phenos tmp.pheno
mv new_phenos.temp ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sims.pheno
```

### Script: `bin/check_vp.py`

| Function | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `get_vp()` | Reads the `check_vp.hsq` file and extracts the Vp value from the row where Source == "Vp" |
| `increase_pheno_var()` | Multiplies all phenotype values (column 3) by 1000 |
| `read_original_phenos()` | Reads the phenotype file unchanged |
| `write_phenos_to_temp()` | Writes output to `new_phenos.temp` |

### Variance check logic

1. Parse `check_vp.hsq` to extract the `Vp` value
2. If `Vp < 0.000001`: multiply all phenotype values by 1000 to increase
   variance
3. If `Vp >= 0.000001`: retain original phenotype values without modification
4. Write output to `new_phenos.temp`, then rename to the final
   `*_sims.pheno` filename

If the `.hsq` file is missing or Vp cannot be parsed, the script falls back to
using the original phenotype values.

### Example `.pheno` output

From test data (`data/test_data/.../PYTHON_CHECK_VP/5_1_0.2_0.05_gamma_ce.96.allout15_irrepressible.grosbeak_sims.pheno`):

```
MY2713 MY2713 -6.04369
JU323 JU323 11.1615
XZ1734 XZ1734 4.15316
QG4080 QG4080 9.589
```

In this case Vp was well above the threshold, so phenotype values are
unchanged from the GCTA simulation output.

### Inputs / Outputs

| Direction | File | Description |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| Input | `tmp.pheno` | Phenotype file (staged from `*_sims.phen`) |
| Input | `check_vp.hsq` | REML variance estimates from `GCTA_MAKE_GRM` |
| Input | `*_sims.par` | Simulation parameter file (passed through) |
| Input | `bin/check_vp.py` | Python script |
| Output | `*_sims.pheno` | Variance-checked phenotype file |
| Output | `*_sims.par` | Simulation parameter file (passed through) |
| Output | GRM + PLINK files | Passed through for downstream use |


## 3. GCTA_PERFORM_GWA

**Source:** `modules/gcta/perform_gwa/main.nf`

Performs genome-wide association mapping. This process receives the GRM and
variance-checked phenotype data, then executes three sub-steps controlled by
the `mode` and `type` parameters.

### Step 1: Create sparse GRM

This step always runs regardless of mode or type:

```bash
gcta64 --grm TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_gcta_grm_${mode} \
    --make-bK-sparse ${sparse_cut} \
    --out ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sparse_grm_${mode} \
    --thread-num 1
```

| Flag | Purpose | Docs |
|------|---------|------|
| `--make-bK-sparse` | Set all off-diagonal GRM values with absolute value &le; `sparse_cut` (default: 0.05) to zero | [GCTA GRM](https://yanglab.westlake.edu.cn/software/gcta/#MakingaGRM) |

This produces a sparse representation of the GRM, reducing memory usage for
the association mapping step.

### Step 2: Conditional PCA

The `type` parameter controls whether PCA eigenvectors are extracted as
covariates:

```bash
if [[ ${type} == "pca" ]]; then
    gcta64 --grm TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_gcta_grm_${mode} \
        --pca 1 \
        --out ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sparse_grm_${mode} \
        --thread-num 1

    COVAR="--qcovar ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sparse_grm_${mode}.eigenvec"
else
    COVAR=""
fi
```

- **`type == "pca"`**: Extracts the first principal component from the GRM
  (`--pca 1`), producing an `.eigenvec` file. This eigenvector is passed as a
  quantitative covariate (`--qcovar`) to the mapping command to control for
  population structure.
- **`type == "nopca"`**: No PCA is performed. The `COVAR` variable is set to
  an empty string, so no covariate is included in the mapping command.

### Step 3: Association mapping

The `mode` parameter determines both the GCTA mapping algorithm and the GRM
input flag:

```bash
if [[ ${mode} == "inbred" ]]; then
    GRM_OPTION='--grm-sparse'
    COMMAND='--fastGWA-mlm-exact'
else
    GRM_OPTION="--grm"
    COMMAND="--mlma-loco"
fi

GWA_THREADS=$( [[ ${mode} == "loco" ]] && echo ${task.cpus} || echo 1 )

gcta64 ${COMMAND} \
    --bfile TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group} \
    ${GRM_OPTION} ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sparse_grm_${mode} \
    ${COVAR} \
    --out ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_lmm-exact_${mode}_${type} \
    --pheno ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sims.pheno \
    --maf ${maf} \
    --thread-num ${GWA_THREADS}
```

> **Thread count:** `--fastGWA-mlm-exact` (inbred mode) uses `--thread-num 1` for
> deterministic results. `--mlma-loco` (loco mode) uses `--thread-num ${task.cpus}`,
> set via the `GWA_THREADS` shell variable, because its per-chromosome refits are
> independent and numerically less sensitive. See [Thread-count determinism](development.qmd#thread-count-determinism).

**Inbred mode (`mode == "inbred"`):**

- Uses `--fastGWA-mlm-exact` to perform an exact mixed linear model (MLM)
  association analysis
  ([GCTA fastGWA docs](https://yanglab.westlake.edu.cn/software/gcta/#fastGWA))
- Uses `--grm-sparse` to input the sparse GRM created from the inbred
  relatedness matrix
- Output format: `.fastGWA` (columns: CHR, SNP, POS, A1, A2, N, AF1, BETA,
  SE, P)

**LOCO mode (`mode == "loco"`):**

- Uses `--mlma-loco` to perform MLM-based association using a
  leave-one-chromosome-out approach, where the GRM excludes the chromosome of
  the tested variant
  ([GCTA MLMA docs](https://yanglab.westlake.edu.cn/software/gcta/#MLMA))
- Uses `--grm` to input the sparse GRM created from the standard relatedness
  matrix
- Output format: `.loco.mlma` (columns: Chr, SNP, bp, A1, A2, Freq, b, se, p),
  renamed to `.mlma` after completion

```bash
if [[ ${mode} == "loco" ]]; then
    mv "${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_lmm-exact_${mode}_${type}.loco.mlma" \
       "${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_lmm-exact_${mode}_${type}.mlma"
fi
```

### Summary of the four mapping conditions

| Mode | Type | GRM Construction | GWA Command | GRM Flag | Covariate | Output Format |
|------|------|------------------|-------------|----------|-----------|---------------|
| inbred | pca | `--make-grm-inbred` | `--fastGWA-mlm-exact` | `--grm-sparse` | `--qcovar .eigenvec` | `.fastGWA` |
| inbred | nopca | `--make-grm-inbred` | `--fastGWA-mlm-exact` | `--grm-sparse` | none | `.fastGWA` |
| loco | pca | `--make-grm` | `--mlma-loco` | `--grm` | `--qcovar .eigenvec` | `.mlma` |
| loco | nopca | `--make-grm` | `--mlma-loco` | `--grm` | none | `.mlma` |

### Mapping identifiers

Each of the four mapping conditions is assigned a unique `mapping_id` by
`write_gwa_to_db.R` during database migration. The ID is a 20-character
SHA-256 hash computed from the parent trait hash and the two condition
parameters:

```
mapping_id = SHA-256("v=1|parent={trait_hash}|algorithm={algorithm}|pca={pca_str}")[0:20]
```

where:

- `{trait_hash}` is the 20-char parent `trait_id` hash (itself derived from
  `marker_set_id`)
- `{algorithm}` is the lowercased `mode` value: `"inbred"` or `"loco"`
- `{pca_str}` is `"TRUE"` if `type == "pca"`, otherwise `"FALSE"`

The full hash hierarchy is:

```
marker_set_id  (population + maf)
  └── trait_id (marker_set_id + nqtl + effect + rep + h2)
        └── mapping_id (trait_id + algorithm + pca)
```

Because each level embeds its parent hash, a `mapping_id` uniquely encodes
the entire simulation and mapping parameter chain. `generate_mapping_id()`
normalizes `algorithm` to lowercase via `tolower()`. The canonical `algorithm`
values are `"inbred"` and `"loco"`, matching `val(mode)` from this process
after normalization.

When mapping results are written to the database, only measurement columns and
foreign keys are stored per row:

| Column | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `marker_set_id` | Hash join key to marker set |
| `trait_id` | Hash join key to parent trait |
| `mapping_id` | Hash join key to mapping metadata |
| `marker` | Marker ID (`CHROM:POS` format) |
| `AF1` | Effect allele frequency |
| `BETA` | Effect size estimate |
| `SE` | Standard error |
| `P` | P-value |

Simulation parameters (`nqtl`, `rep`, `h2`, `effect`, `algorithm`, `pca`,
`population`, `maf`) are stored once in `mappings_metadata.parquet` and
recovered via FK joins — they are not repeated in every data row.

### Example `.fastGWA` output

First 10 lines from test data (`data/test_data/.../GCTA_PERFORM_GWA/*_lmm-exact_inbred_pca.fastGWA`):

```
CHR	SNP	POS	A1	A2	N	AF1	BETA	SE	P
1	1:4559	4559	A	G	96	0.229167	1.00868	1.84192	0.583949
1	1:6313	6313	G	T	96	0.197917	1.98669	1.9353	0.304629
1	1:6580	6580	T	A	96	0.260417	4.48856	1.70508	0.00847695
1	1:6689	6689	G	A	96	0.489583	-4.33545	1.48526	0.0035117
1	1:9328	9328	G	A	96	0.28125	0.0688625	1.72457	0.968149
1	1:13117	13117	A	C	96	0.135417	-8.29242	2.0985	7.76314e-05
1	1:15024	15024	A	G	96	0.15625	0.399956	2.13512	0.851407
1	1:15858	15858	G	C	96	0.104167	0.271408	2.53814	0.914843
1	1:18223	18223	C	T	96	0.0625	-0.675012	3.20252	0.833063
```

| Column | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| CHR | Chromosome |
| SNP | Marker ID (`CHROM:POS` format) |
| POS | Base pair position |
| A1 | Effect allele |
| A2 | Other allele |
| N | Sample size |
| AF1 | Frequency of the effect allele |
| BETA | Effect size estimate |
| SE | Standard error of BETA |
| P | P-value for the association test |

### Inputs / Outputs

| Direction | File | Description |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| Input | `*_gcta_grm_${mode}.grm.bin/.grm.N.bin/.grm.id` | GRM files from `GCTA_MAKE_GRM` |
| Input | `TO_SIMS_*.bed/.bim/.fam` | Phenotype-annotated PLINK binary fileset |
| Input | `*_sims.pheno` + `*_sims.par` | Variance-checked phenotype and parameter files from `PYTHON_CHECK_VP` |
| Input | `sparse_cut` (val) | Sparse GRM threshold (default: 0.05) |
| Input | `mode` (val) | `"inbred"` or `"loco"` |
| Input | `type` (val) | `"pca"` or `"nopca"` |
| Output | `*_lmm-exact_${mode}_${type}.${suffix}` | Association results (`.fastGWA` for inbred, `.mlma` for loco) |
| Output | GRM + PLINK + phenotype files | Passed through for downstream use |

> **Database migration:** After this process, `write_gwa_to_db.R` reads the
> association result file and writes it to the database using the mapping
> identifier scheme described above. `mode` is passed as `algorithm` (normalized
> to lowercase by `generate_mapping_id()`). `type` is evaluated as
> `type == "pca"` to produce an R logical, which is then serialized as `"TRUE"`
> or `"FALSE"` in the hash string.

### Thread-count determinism

All GCTA steps except `--mlma-loco` are pinned to `--thread-num 1` to ensure
reproducible floating-point results; see [Thread-count determinism](development.qmd#thread-count-determinism)
in the developer notes for the full rationale.
